mnemonics.title: Gradient Points Steepest Ascent
mnemonics.content: G-P-S-A: Gradient Points Steepest Ascent - Remember that gradient ∇f always points in the direction where function increases fastest. For descent, go opposite!

mnemonics.title: Critical Point Checklist
mnemonics.content: C-P-S: Critical Points found by Setting gradient to zero - ∇f = 0 means solve all partial derivatives equal to zero.

mnemonics.title: Hessian Classification
mnemonics.content: P-N-I: Positive definite = Minimum, Negative definite = Maximum, Indefinite = Saddle. Always check determinant and eigenvalues.

mnemonics.title: Lagrange Method Steps
mnemonics.content: L-O-G: Lagrange multiply constraint to objective, Optimize (differentiate), Get equations - solve ∇f = λ∇g together with g(x)=0.

acronyms.expansion: Hessian: Second partial derivatives matrix - E = Expected curvature information, S = Symmetric (if continuous), S = Second order, I = Information (curvature), A = Always used for classification, N = Necessary for sufficiency test

Jacobian: Just Another Collection Of First-order Partial derivatives - J = First derivative matrix, A = All component functions, C = Contains ∂fi/∂xj elements, O = Organized in matrix form, B = Bridges single to multi-variable

Lagrange: Lagrangian function L = f - λg - L = Objective minus lambda times constraint, A = Add gradient equation ∇L = 0, G = Get system of equations, R = Resolve unknowns and λ, A = Always satisfy constraint, N = Note multiplier meaning, G = Gradient parallel condition, E = Evaluate optimum point

visualTips.visualization: Imagine gradient as an arrow at each point on a hill - pointing uphill. The steeper the hill, the longer the arrow. Contour lines are like topographic maps; gradient is perpendicular to them.
Think of a horse saddle - it curves UP toward the horse's head and tail, but DOWN toward the sides. This is like f(x,y) = y² - x² at origin.
Imagine standing on a hill (surface). The directional derivative is how fast you're ascending if you walk in a specific direction. Walking in gradient direction = fastest ascent.
Think of Hessian as a 2D version of second derivative - it tells if you're in a valley (positive = holds water), hill (negative = water runs off), or saddle. In 2D, test using D = fxx*fyy - (fxy)².
Draw contour lines (like weather maps). The gradient vector at any point is perpendicular to the level curve passing through that point - pointing toward higher values.

analogies.analogy: Like measuring how your height changes when you only move in the north-south direction while someone holds you fixed in the east-west direction.
Gradient is like a GPS showing the steepest uphill path at your current location. To go down (minimize), follow the GPS in reverse direction.
Critical points are like locations where the land is perfectly flat (no slope) - could be at the top of a hill (maximum), bottom of a valley (minimum), or a mountain pass (saddle).
Lagrange multiplier λ tells you the 'exchange rate' - if you relax your constraint by a tiny bit, how much does your objective function improve? It's like the marginal value of the constraint.
A convex function is like a bowl - no matter where you put a ball, it rolls to the bottom (global minimum). Non-convex functions can have multiple valleys where balls can get stuck in local minima.
